quiz.title: Macroeconomics (Quiz 6)
quiz.subtitle: Unit 5 Quiz 6: Balanced Budget Multiplier (20 Questions)
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: Fiscal Policy, Balanced Budget Multiplier, Government Spending, Taxation

questions.question: The balanced budget multiplier is always equal to:
questions.explanation: Regardless of the MPC, an equal increase in government spending and taxes increases GDP by exactly the amount of the spending increase. The balanced budget multiplier is always 1.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.8. Government spending and taxes both increase by \$50 billion. The net effect on GDP is:
questions.explanation: The balanced budget multiplier equals 1. Net effect = +\$50 billion.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.6. Government spending and taxes both decrease by \$30 billion. GDP will:
questions.explanation: BBM = 1. The change in GDP equals the change in government spending: -\$30 billion.

questions.question: The MPS is 0.1. Government spending and taxes both rise by \$75 billion. GDP changes by:
questions.explanation: BBM = 1 regardless of MPS or MPC. ΔGDP = +\$75 billion.

questions.question: If the government increases both spending and taxes by \$100 billion, the net effect on GDP is +\$100 billion. This demonstrates that:
options: The spending multiplier equals the tax multiplier
questions.explanation: An equal increase in G and T raises GDP by exactly ΔG. This is the definition of the balanced budget multiplier equaling 1.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.75. To increase GDP by \$200 billion using a balanced budget approach, spending and taxes must each:
questions.explanation: ΔGDP = ΔG (since BBM = 1). Required ΔG = \$200 billion. Balanced budget means ΔT = \$200 billion as well.

questions.question: The MPS is 0.25. Government increases G by \$40 billion and T by \$40 billion. Which statement is correct?
options: The spending multiplier is 4, so GDP rises by \$160 billion
The tax multiplier is -3, so GDP falls by \$120 billion
The net effect is \$0 because the changes offset
questions.explanation: While the individual multiplier effects are large, the net effect is simply +\$40 billion. The balanced budget multiplier equals 1.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.9. If government spending and taxes both increase by \$25 billion, consumption spending will initially:
questions.explanation: The tax hike reduces disposable income by \$25 billion. Initial change in consumption = -\$25B × MPC = -\$25B × 0.9 = -\$22.5 billion.

questions.question: A balanced budget increase of \$60 billion in G and T will shift the AD curve:
questions.explanation: BBM = 1. The AD curve shifts right by exactly \$60 billion at every price level.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.5. Government increases G by \$80 billion and T by \$80 billion. The total change in GDP is:
questions.explanation: BBM = 1. ΔGDP = +\$80 billion.

questions.question: To close a recessionary gap of \$150 billion using a balanced budget fiscal policy, the government must:
options: Increase G by \$150 billion and T by \$0
Increase G by \$150 billion and T by \$150 billion
Increase G by \$75 billion and T by \$75 billion
Decrease G by \$150 billion and T by \$150 billion
questions.explanation: ΔGDP = ΔG. Required ΔG = \$150 billion. Balanced budget means ΔT = \$150 billion as well.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.8. Government increases G by \$50 billion and T by \$50 billion. The spending effect alone adds ______, and the tax effect alone subtracts ______.
questions.explanation: Spending multiplier = 5 → +\$250B. Tax multiplier = -4 → -\$200B. Net = \$250B - \$200B = +\$50B.

questions.question: The balanced budget multiplier equals 1 because:
options: The spending multiplier is exactly one unit larger in absolute value than the tax multiplier
Government spending has no effect on GDP
Taxes have no effect on GDP
questions.explanation: |Spending mult| - |Tax mult| = 1/(1-MPC) - MPC/(1-MPC) = (1-MPC)/(1-MPC) = 1. The spending multiplier always exceeds the absolute tax multiplier by exactly 1.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.6. A balanced budget decrease of \$45 billion in G and T will cause GDP to:
questions.explanation: BBM = 1. ΔGDP = ΔG = -\$45 billion.

questions.question: If the government wants to reduce GDP by \$100 billion using a balanced budget contraction, it must:
options: Cut G by \$100 billion and raise T by \$100 billion
Cut G by \$100 billion and cut T by \$100 billion
Raise G by \$100 billion and cut T by \$100 billion
Raise G by \$100 billion and raise T by \$100 billion
questions.explanation: To reduce GDP by \$100B, ΔG = -\$100B. Balanced budget requires ΔT = -\$100B (both cut equally).

questions.question: The MPS is 0.3. A balanced budget increase of \$90 billion in G and T will increase GDP by:
questions.explanation: BBM = 1. ΔGDP = +\$90 billion, regardless of the MPS or MPC.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.75. If G and T both increase by \$120 billion, the net effect on the government budget deficit is:
questions.explanation: Balanced budget: ΔG = ΔT, so ΔDeficit = ΔG - ΔT = \$0. The deficit remains unchanged.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.8. A balanced budget increase of \$35 billion occurs. The spending multiplier effect is +\$175 billion. The tax multiplier effect is:
questions.explanation: Tax multiplier = -4. ΔT = +\$35B. Effect = -4 × \$35B = -\$140 billion.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly proves the balanced budget multiplier equals 1?
questions.explanation: Spending multiplier - |Tax multiplier| = 1/(1-MPC) - MPC/(1-MPC) = (1-MPC)/(1-MPC) = 1.

questions.question: The MPC is 0.5. If the government wants to increase GDP by \$200 billion without changing the budget deficit, it should:
options: Increase both G and T by \$200 billion
Increase G by \$100 billion and T by \$100 billion
Decrease G by \$200 billion and T by \$200 billion
questions.explanation: To leave the deficit unchanged, ΔG must equal ΔT. BBM = 1, so ΔGDP = ΔG = \$200B. Both must increase by \$200 billion.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the balanced budget multiplier and understand why it always equals 1.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the core concept. Remember: Net ΔGDP = ΔG (or ΔT) regardless of the MPC.
scoreMessages.50: A fair attempt. Focus on: 1) BBM always equals 1. 2) ΔGDP = ΔG when budget is balanced. 3) The spending multiplier always exceeds the tax multiplier by exactly 1.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Remember: An equal increase in G and T is expansionary because government spending injects money directly, while a tax hike only reduces spending by the MPC fraction.
